meanings.definition: A record or statement of money received and spent, or of business transactions.
examples: The treasurer kept careful accounts of every dollar the club spent.
She reviewed the company's accounts before the audit.

meanings.definition: A description or report of an event or experience.
examples: He gave a detailed account of what happened during the storm.

meanings.definition: To explain or give a reason for something.
examples: She could not account for the missing files.

meanings.definition: Having limits or bounds; not infinite.
examples: We have only a finite amount of time to finish the project.
The earth holds a finite supply of fossil fuels.

meanings.definition: Ordinary, dull, and lacking imagination or poetic feeling.
examples: His prosaic description of the sunset failed to capture its beauty.
The meeting covered prosaic matters like parking and supplies.

meanings.definition: To the fullest extent; completely or entirely.
examples: I am not quite finished with my homework.
You are quite right about the answer.

meanings.definition: To a moderate but noticeable degree; fairly.
examples: The film was quite good, though not her best work.

meanings.definition: Sedate, serious, and steady in character or manner.
examples: The staid old banker rarely showed any emotion.
Their staid lifestyle left little room for spontaneity.

meanings.definition: The state of being dormant, hidden, or not yet active.
examples: The virus can remain in a state of latency for years before causing symptoms.

meanings.definition: The delay between an action and the response to it.
examples: High network latency made the video call lag noticeably.

meanings.definition: A playful leap, skip, or frisking movement.
examples: The lambs broke into a joyful gambol across the meadow.

meanings.definition: To run, leap, or skip about playfully.
examples: Children gamboled in the park after the rain stopped.

meanings.definition: Completely and entirely; without reservation.
examples: She rejected the offer outright.
He owns the house outright, with no mortgage.

meanings.definition: At once; immediately.
examples: The crash killed the driver outright.

meanings.definition: Having sharp corners or angles.
examples: The modern building had an angular, geometric design.

meanings.definition: Lean and bony in physical form.
examples: His angular face and tall frame made him easy to spot in a crowd.

meanings.definition: Patient courage and strength of mind in facing pain or difficulty.
examples: She bore the long illness with remarkable fortitude.
It took great fortitude to keep going after so many setbacks.

meanings.definition: To value or estimate someone or something at less than its true worth.
examples: Critics tend to underrate her early novels.
Don't underrate the difficulty of the climb.

meanings.definition: An official count of the people of a country or region, often gathering related statistics.
examples: The national census is taken every ten years.
The census showed that the city's population had doubled.

meanings.definition: Fit or suitable to be lived in.
examples: After the repairs, the old farmhouse was finally habitable.
Scientists search for habitable planets beyond our solar system.

meanings.definition: To give up or set aside a right or claim, especially voluntarily.
examples: The landlord agreed to waive the late fee this month.
She waived her right to a lawyer during questioning.

meanings.definition: A person who earns a living through manual labor.
examples: The new policy was meant to protect the ordinary working-man.
As a working-man, he valued an honest day's pay.

meanings.definition: Irreligious, or characteristic of those regarded as having no religion.
examples: The missionaries condemned what they called the heathenish customs of the region.
His heathenish disregard for tradition shocked the elders.

meanings.definition: Any of various tall, rush-like plants that grow in damp ground or shallow water.
examples: The baby Moses was hidden among the bulrushes by the river.
Frogs croaked from the bulrushes along the pond's edge.

meanings.definition: An advanced level of skill or knowledge in a particular field or activity.
examples: She demonstrated great proficiency in three foreign languages.
Years of practice gave him real proficiency at the piano.

meanings.definition: A thin board, with a thumb hole, on which a painter mixes and holds colors.
examples: The artist squeezed fresh paint onto her palette.

meanings.definition: The range of colors used by an artist or in a particular work.
examples: The painting used a warm palette of reds and golds.

meanings.definition: Making little or no sound; silent.
examples: The cat crept across the floor with noiseless steps.
The new fan ran almost noiseless in the quiet room.

meanings.definition: A person who measures and maps land, its boundaries, and features.
examples: The surveyor marked the exact corners of the property.
A team of surveyors mapped the route for the new highway.

meanings.definition: The quality of being neat, tidy, and in good order.
examples: The trimness of the garden showed how carefully it was tended.
She admired the trimness of his well-kept uniform.

meanings.definition: Protected from or not affected by something, especially a disease.
examples: Once you've had the illness, you are usually immune to it.
Vaccination made the children immune to the virus.

meanings.definition: Exempt from a particular obligation, penalty, or influence.
examples: No one is immune from criticism in this business.

meanings.definition: Serving as evidence; pointing to or indicating something.
examples: The fingerprints had strong evidential value in the trial.
Her hesitation was evidential of her uncertainty.

meanings.definition: To make someone or something seem less important or worthy; to disparage.
examples: He constantly belittled his coworkers' ideas.
Don't belittle your own achievements; they matter.

meanings.definition: To bind someone, legally or morally, to fulfill a duty or obligation.
examples: The contract obligates the company to deliver by June.
Nothing obligates you to attend the meeting.

meanings.definition: Not able to be denied or disputed; certain.
examples: The DNA results were incontrovertible proof of his innocence.
Her argument rested on incontrovertible facts.

meanings.definition: Having the grace, poise, or stillness of a statue.
examples: The dancer held a statuesque pose at the end of the performance.
Her statuesque beauty drew every eye in the room.

meanings.definition: Awkward and clumsy in movement or appearance.
examples: The young colt took its first ungainly steps.
He felt ungainly and out of place on the dance floor.

meanings.definition: Capable of causing or deserving laughter; laughable.
examples: His risible excuse for being late made everyone smile.
The plot was so risible that the audience laughed out loud.

meanings.definition: To work out or determine by mathematical calculation.
examples: The software can compute the total cost in seconds.
She computed the average score for the whole class.

meanings.definition: The state of being a father; fatherhood.
examples: He happily accepted the responsibilities of paternity.
A test confirmed the paternity of the child.

meanings.definition: To moisten meat with fat, juices, or gravy while it cooks.
examples: Baste the turkey every half hour to keep it juicy.
She basted the roast with its own drippings.

meanings.definition: Serving to assist or support; auxiliary.
examples: The adjutant forces arrived to reinforce the main army.
His adjutant role kept the operation running smoothly.

meanings.definition: A military officer who assists a commanding officer with administrative duties.
examples: The colonel sent his adjutant to deliver the orders.

meanings.definition: Able to be made dishonest or won over by bribery.
examples: A corruptible official can undermine an entire government.
They believed every judge was corruptible for the right price.

meanings.definition: Special honor, respect, or reverence shown publicly to someone or something.
examples: The crowd paid homage to the fallen soldiers.
The film is an homage to the directors she admired.

meanings.definition: Showing a willingness to take bold risks; fearless.
examples: Her audacious plan to cross the desert alone amazed everyone.
It was an audacious move that no one expected.

meanings.definition: Boldly disrespectful or impudent.
examples: The audacious remark left his teacher speechless.

meanings.definition: To represent something as larger, better, or worse than it really is; to overstate.
examples: He tends to exaggerate the size of the fish he catches.
Don't exaggerate; the wait wasn't that long.

meanings.definition: In poetry, a line of verse containing five metrical feet.
examples: Shakespeare wrote much of his work in iambic pentameter.
Each line of the sonnet was a flawless pentameter.

meanings.definition: Resembling a tree, especially in branching form.
examples: The frost formed dendroid patterns across the window.
The mineral grew in a dendroid, branching shape.

meanings.definition: Stubborn and unyielding persistence in a course, belief, or purpose.
examples: Her pertinacity in pursuing the case eventually won justice.
He argued his point with surprising pertinacity.

meanings.definition: Something that follows as a result or continuation of what came before.
examples: The novel's sequel continues the hero's journey.
The lawsuit was an unfortunate sequel to their disagreement.

meanings.definition: Happening once every seven years, or lasting seven years.
examples: The town holds a septennial festival to mark the anniversary.
The treaty established a septennial review of the borders.

meanings.definition: To impose and collect a tax, fee, or fine, often by force of law.
examples: The government levied a new tax on imported goods.
Officials levied heavy fines on polluting factories.

meanings.definition: An amount of money raised or a tax imposed by law.
examples: A small levy was added to fund local schools.

meanings.definition: An image, picture, or representation of something or someone.
examples: The ancient icon depicted a saint surrounded by gold.

meanings.definition: A small symbol on a screen that represents a program, file, or function.
examples: Click the printer icon to start the print job.

meanings.definition: A person or thing widely admired as a symbol of something.
examples: She became a cultural icon for an entire generation.

meanings.definition: A part of the body or thing that is misshapen or disfigured.
examples: Surgery corrected the deformity in the child's spine.
The accident left a permanent deformity in the metal frame.

meanings.definition: Able to be perceived by touch; physically real.
examples: The museum displayed tangible artifacts from the dig.

meanings.definition: Clear and definite enough to be noticed or measured.
examples: The new policy brought tangible benefits to workers.

meanings.definition: A raised structure or place where sacrifices are offered or worship is performed.
examples: The priest lit candles upon the altar.
They knelt before the altar to exchange their vows.

meanings.definition: A person or company to whom goods or property are delivered or entrusted.
examples: The shipment was delivered directly to the consignee.
As consignee, she signed for the arriving crates.

meanings.definition: A person who wanders from place to place without a home or steady work.
examples: A vagrant slept on the bench in the city square.

meanings.definition: Wandering or roaming without a settled course.
examples: A vagrant breeze drifted through the open window.

meanings.definition: Involving or determined by right angles; perpendicular.
examples: The two streets meet in an orthogonal grid.
The lines are orthogonal, crossing at ninety degrees.

meanings.definition: Independent of or unrelated to something else.
examples: Those two issues are orthogonal and should be discussed separately.

meanings.definition: To recover, especially in health or strength after illness or loss.
examples: She took a week off to recuperate from the surgery.
The company struggled to recuperate after the heavy losses.

meanings.definition: Relating to or possessing wisdom.
examples: The elder offered sapiential advice gathered over a lifetime.
Her sapiential insight guided the younger students.

meanings.definition: To fail to understand correctly; to misunderstand.
examples: I'm afraid you misapprehend my intentions.
They misapprehended the instructions and started over.

meanings.definition: Capable of being poured or transferred from one vessel or body to another.
examples: The liquid was easily transfusible between the two flasks.
Certain blood types are transfusible only to matching recipients.

meanings.definition: Without color; colorless.
examples: The drawing was rendered in achromatic shades of gray.
An achromatic lens transmits light without splitting it into colors.

meanings.definition: Unwilling to give or share; stingy.
examples: His illiberal attitude toward tipping embarrassed his friends.
She was illiberal with praise, even when it was deserved.

meanings.definition: Narrow-minded or intolerant of other views.
examples: The regime grew increasingly illiberal toward free speech.

meanings.definition: Expressing or relating to a cause or the relationship between cause and effect.
examples: Researchers found a causal link between smoking and disease.
The word 'because' introduces a causal clause.

meanings.definition: Suitability or usefulness for achieving a particular purpose, often regardless of principle.
examples: The decision was made out of political expediency rather than fairness.
They chose the cheaper route as a matter of expediency.

meanings.definition: To revive someone from unconsciousness or apparent death.
examples: Paramedics managed to resuscitate the drowning swimmer.
Doctors worked quickly to resuscitate the patient.

meanings.definition: To bring something back into use or activity.
examples: The new manager hoped to resuscitate the failing business.

meanings.definition: To take hold of something suddenly and forcibly.
examples: She seized the rope before it slipped away.
He seized the opportunity to introduce himself.

meanings.definition: To take possession of something by legal authority or force.
examples: Police seized the smuggled goods at the border.

meanings.definition: Happening or done at once, with no perceptible delay.
examples: The response from the server was nearly instantaneous.
His reaction to the danger was instantaneous.

meanings.definition: The meaning conveyed by a word, phrase, or action.
examples: In what sense are you using the word 'fair'?
The poem can be read in more than one sense.

meanings.definition: A faculty by which the body perceives the world, such as sight or hearing.
examples: The smell of rain awakened her sense of nostalgia.

meanings.definition: Sound practical judgment.
examples: He had the good sense to wear a coat in the storm.

meanings.definition: Fairness and impartiality in the way people are treated.
examples: The new policy aims to promote equity among all employees.
Judges are expected to rule with equity.

meanings.definition: The value of an owner's share in property after debts are subtracted.
examples: They built up significant equity in their home over the years.

meanings.definition: To handle or manage something badly, improperly, or carelessly.
examples: The director mismanaged the budget and the project collapsed.
They accused the board of mismanaging the company's funds.

meanings.definition: Exercising power in a cruel, oppressive, and arbitrary way; despotic.
examples: The tyrannical ruler crushed all opposition.
His tyrannical management left employees afraid to speak up.

meanings.definition: To greatly reduce or remove the population of a place.
examples: The famine depopulated entire villages.
War and disease depopulated the once-thriving region.

meanings.definition: Soft and spongy in texture, like a fungus.
examples: The rotting log had a fungous, spongy surface.
A fungous growth spread over the damp wall.

meanings.definition: To make something necessary or unavoidable as a result.
examples: The heavy rain necessitated a change in our plans.
Growth will necessitate hiring more staff.

meanings.definition: To completely remove or get rid of something.
examples: The new process eliminated several unnecessary steps.
We need to eliminate waste from the system.

meanings.definition: To exclude or knock out from a competition or consideration.
examples: Their team was eliminated in the first round.

meanings.definition: Relating to or suited to a feast or celebration; joyous and festive.
examples: The hall was decked with lights and flowers for the festal occasion.
Their festal mood lasted well into the night.

meanings.definition: Capable of being reflected, as light or heat.
examples: The polished surface made the beam highly reflexible.
Engineers tested how reflexible the new coating was under sunlight.

meanings.definition: Harmful to living things; injurious to health or morals.
examples: Noxious fumes from the factory drifted over the town.
The gas leak released a noxious cloud that forced an evacuation.

meanings.definition: A statement foretelling what will happen in the future.
examples: Her prediction that it would rain proved correct.
The economist's prediction of a downturn worried investors.

meanings.definition: An exclamation of approval, meaning well done or excellent.
examples: "Bravo!" the audience shouted as the singer took her bow.
Bravo, you solved the puzzle faster than anyone.

meanings.definition: A word, phrase, or usage characteristic of or originating in American English.
examples: The word "elevator" is an Americanism, where the British say "lift".
Editors flagged several Americanisms in the British edition of the book.

meanings.definition: To concentrate on or become an expert in a particular area or activity.
examples: She decided to specialize in pediatric medicine.
The shop specializes in rare and antique books.

meanings.definition: Deserving respect, admiration, or high regard.
examples: He was an estimable scholar admired by his peers.
Her estimable conduct earned the trust of the whole community.

meanings.definition: To increase the power, status, wealth, or reputation of someone or something.
examples: The ruler waged war only to aggrandize his empire.
He used every speech to aggrandize his own achievements.

meanings.definition: An individual example of something, especially one taken as representative of a class or group.
examples: The museum displayed a rare specimen of a giant beetle.
She collected a blood specimen for the laboratory tests.

meanings.definition: Capable of being turned or thrown back, as light or sound.
examples: The mirror's coating made the rays easily reflectible.
They studied which materials are most reflectible to radar.

meanings.definition: A person who lingers idly or wastes time in a place without purpose.
examples: The guard moved a loiterer along from the shop entrance.
A few loiterers stood on the corner long after the store closed.

meanings.definition: Reckless and shameless extravagance or moral abandon.
examples: His profligacy soon emptied the family fortune.
The court was notorious for its luxury and profligacy.

meanings.definition: Foolish, clownish, or ridiculous behavior intended to amuse.
examples: The clown's buffoonery had the children shrieking with laughter.
Critics dismissed the speech as mere political buffoonery.

meanings.definition: A large French country house, castle, or manor, often with grounds and a vineyard.
examples: They spent the summer at a chateau in the Loire Valley.
The wine bore the name of the chateau where its grapes were grown.

meanings.definition: An instrument for measuring the intensity of light or comparing two light sources.
examples: The astronomer used a photometer to measure the star's brightness.
A photometer confirmed that the new bulb shone brighter than the old one.

meanings.definition: Fearless, bold, and undaunted in the face of danger.
examples: The intrepid explorer crossed the frozen wasteland alone.
She was an intrepid reporter who covered the war from the front lines.

meanings.definition: The state of being extremely poor and dependent on charity or public support.
examples: Reformers sought to relieve the pauperism of the slums.
Widespread pauperism followed the collapse of local industry.

meanings.definition: An established or official way of doing something, carried out in a series of steps.
examples: The nurse explained the procedure before the operation began.
Follow the correct procedure when filing your application.

meanings.definition: Extreme poverty or want.
examples: The family lived in penury after the harvest failed.
Years of penury had taught him to value every coin.

meanings.definition: Clever and skillful, especially in using the hands or mind.
examples: She gave an adroit answer that satisfied everyone.
The adroit juggler kept six balls in the air at once.

meanings.definition: Inflammation of the bronchial tubes, causing coughing and difficulty breathing.
examples: The damp winter left him with a stubborn case of bronchitis.
Smoking greatly increases the risk of chronic bronchitis.

meanings.definition: Recovering one's health and strength after an illness or injury.
examples: The convalescent patient was finally allowed to walk in the garden.
She spent two weeks at a convalescent home by the sea.

meanings.definition: To shout or cry out loudly and forcefully.
examples: The crowd began to vociferate their demands at the gates.
He vociferated his objections until the whole room fell silent.

meanings.definition: A person who has broken the law and is on the run or living outside its protection.
examples: The outlaw fled into the hills to escape the sheriff.
Old ballads tell of an outlaw who robbed the rich to feed the poor.

meanings.definition: A person who gives money or other help to a person or cause.
examples: An anonymous benefactor paid for the new hospital wing.
The students thanked the benefactor who funded their scholarships.

meanings.definition: A person skilled in logical argument and reasoning.
examples: As a sharp dialectician, she could dismantle any weak argument.
The philosopher was admired as a brilliant dialectician.

meanings.definition: A class or social group set apart by hereditary rank, profession, or wealth.
examples: In some societies a person's caste determines their occupation.
She resented the rigid caste that kept the wealthy apart from the poor.

meanings.definition: To use up or pay out, especially money, time, or energy.
examples: They expended a great deal of effort on the failed project.
Do not expend all your savings on a single purchase.

meanings.definition: A sudden, unpredictable change of mind or behavior; a whim.
examples: On a caprice, she booked a flight to Rome that evening.
His decisions seemed driven by caprice rather than reason.

meanings.definition: Showing intense and eager interest or excitement.
examples: The team was enthusiastic about the new plan.
She gave an enthusiastic cheer when her name was called.

meanings.definition: Very complicated or detailed, with many interrelated parts.
examples: The clock had an intricate mechanism of tiny gears.
She wove an intricate pattern into the tapestry.

meanings.definition: The highest singing voice of a woman or boy, or a singer with such a voice.
examples: The soprano hit the high note with effortless clarity.
She sang the soprano part in the church choir.

meanings.definition: Strikingly bold, showy, or extravagant in style or manner.
examples: He wore a flamboyant purple suit to the party.
Her flamboyant gestures drew every eye on the stage.

meanings.definition: Complete and including nearly everything within its scope.
examples: The report gave a comprehensive overview of the company's finances.
She made a comprehensive list of every task that remained.

meanings.definition: Dishonest or deceitful behavior in dealing with others.
examples: His knavery was exposed when the forged papers were found.
The merchant's knavery cheated dozens of unsuspecting customers.

meanings.definition: The act of leaving something out, or the thing left out.
examples: The omission of her name from the list was an honest mistake.
A single omission in the report changed its entire meaning.

meanings.definition: Shockingly vivid or sensational, especially in a gruesome or scandalous way.
examples: The newspaper printed lurid details of the crime.
He told lurid tales of his adventures to frighten the children.

meanings.definition: Glowing with an unnatural, often reddish or fiery light.
examples: A lurid glow from the fire lit up the night sky.

meanings.definition: To cut or carve a design, letters, or marks into a hard surface.
examples: They asked the jeweler to engrave their initials on the ring.
The artist engraved a delicate pattern into the copper plate.

meanings.definition: In Greek mythology, the many-headed water serpent slain by Hercules that grew new heads when one was cut off.
examples: Hercules burned each neck of the Hydra to stop its heads from regrowing.
Corruption proved to be a Hydra: cut down in one place, it sprang up in another.

meanings.definition: The act of deliberately excluding someone from a group or society.
examples: He faced ostracism from his classmates after the scandal.
Fear of social ostracism kept many people silent.

meanings.definition: To put something in the wrong place and be unable to find it.
examples: I always misplace my keys when I'm in a hurry.
She misplaced the report and spent an hour searching for it.

meanings.definition: The act of going or being put completely under the surface of water or another liquid.
examples: The submergence of the valley followed the building of the dam.
Rising seas threaten the submergence of low-lying islands.

meanings.definition: Bad, improper, or wrongful conduct.
examples: The student was sent home for repeated misbehavior.
Their misbehavior at the meeting embarrassed everyone.

meanings.definition: A person or business that supplies or provides goods, services, or information.
examples: The shop was a well-known purveyor of fine cheeses.
Critics called the channel a purveyor of misinformation.

meanings.definition: An instrument that measures the density or specific gravity of liquids by how it floats in them.
examples: The brewer used a hydrometer to check the sugar content of the wort.
A hydrometer revealed that the battery acid had weakened.

meanings.definition: A person or thing that corresponds to or has the same function as another in a different place or context.
examples: The foreign minister met with her American counterpart.
This drug is the generic counterpart of a costly brand-name version.

meanings.definition: Yes; used to express agreement or assent, especially in voting.
examples: "Aye," said the sailor, ready to obey the order.
All those in favor said aye, and the motion passed.

meanings.definition: A distinctive habit, mannerism, or way of behaving peculiar to an individual.
examples: Tapping his pen while thinking was one of his idiosyncrasies.
Every writer has idiosyncrasies of style that set them apart.

meanings.definition: To calm or lessen the intensity of a fear, concern, or unpleasant feeling.
examples: The doctor's reassuring words helped allay her fears.
Nothing could allay his suspicion that something was wrong.

meanings.definition: The status of belonging to a particular nation by birth or citizenship.
examples: She holds dual nationality, both French and Canadian.
Travelers had to state their nationality at the border.

meanings.definition: A hard, glossy type of coal that burns slowly with little smoke.
examples: They heated the old house with anthracite all winter.
Anthracite gives off more heat and less soot than softer coals.

meanings.definition: Full, complete, and absolute, with no limitations.
examples: The committee was granted plenary powers to act on its own.
Every delegate attended the conference's plenary session.

meanings.definition: Relating to or characteristic of autumn.
examples: The hills were painted in autumnal shades of gold and red.
A cool autumnal breeze rustled the falling leaves.

meanings.definition: To fill someone with intense fear or dread.
examples: The thought of speaking in public terrified him.
The sudden thunderstorm terrified the small children.

meanings.definition: Strong and healthy, especially in old age.
examples: At ninety, my grandfather is still hale and active.
The doctor pronounced her hale and free of illness.

meanings.definition: To speak of or treat someone or something as having little worth; belittle.
examples: She would never disparage a colleague's honest effort.
Critics disparaged the film as shallow and dull.

meanings.definition: Using few words; brief and to the point, sometimes abruptly so.
examples: His terse reply made it clear he wanted no further discussion.
She wrote a terse note saying only "Meeting cancelled."

meanings.definition: The act of washing or cleansing, especially of the body, often as a ritual.
examples: He performed his morning ablutions before prayer.
Ritual ablution is required before entering the temple.

meanings.definition: Clear and easy to understand; expressed plainly.
examples: The professor gave a lucid explanation of the theory.
Her writing is admired for its lucid, simple style.

meanings.definition: Having or showing clear thought, especially during a period of confusion or illness.
examples: The patient had a lucid moment and recognized his daughter.

meanings.definition: Intense anger; wrath.
examples: His careless remark drew the ire of the whole committee.
She trembled at the ire in her father's voice.

meanings.definition: The state of continuing or remaining in existence over time.
examples: The continuance of the project depends on further funding.
They prayed for the continuance of peace between the nations.

meanings.definition: In a very cold or unfriendly manner.
examples: "That is none of your concern," she said icily.
He greeted his rival icily and turned away.

meanings.definition: A person appointed in a will to carry out its terms after the maker's death.
examples: As executor of the estate, she had to settle all the debts.
He named his brother executor of his will.

meanings.definition: Causing a liquid, especially blood, to thicken or clot.
examples: The wound was treated with a coagulant substance to stop the bleeding.
Certain coagulant agents are used to clarify cloudy water.

meanings.definition: A substance that causes coagulation or clotting.
examples: Cheesemakers add a coagulant to turn the milk into curds.

meanings.definition: In feudal times, a person who held land from a lord in return for loyalty and service.
examples: The vassal swore allegiance to his lord and pledged his sword.
Each knight was a vassal bound to fight for the king.

meanings.definition: A person who intervenes on behalf of another, especially to plead or mediate.
examples: She acted as an intercessor between the two feuding families.
The priest was seen as an intercessor between the people and God.

meanings.definition: An original model or first version of something on which later forms are based.
examples: Engineers built a working prototype of the new engine.
The early aircraft served as the prototype for modern jets.

meanings.definition: The act of calling down a curse; a curse spoken against someone.
examples: The witch uttered a malediction upon the careless traveler.
His letter was filled with maledictions against his enemies.

meanings.definition: The beginning or start of something.
examples: The club has grown steadily since its inception.
From its inception, the plan faced fierce opposition.

meanings.definition: Greatest in power, influence, number, or importance; most common or noticeable.
examples: The predominant color in the painting is a deep blue.
Agriculture was the predominant industry in the region.

meanings.definition: To give a vaccine to a person or animal to protect them against a disease.
examples: Doctors urged parents to vaccinate their children against measles.
The clinic vaccinated thousands of people during the flu season.

meanings.definition: In bed; lying in bed.
examples: He was still abed when the visitors arrived.
On rainy mornings she liked to stay abed and read.

meanings.definition: Added to something to make it more complete or to improve it.
examples: The teacher handed out supplementary reading for advanced students.
He took supplementary vitamins to fill gaps in his diet.

meanings.definition: To think about something carefully and for a long time.
examples: She sat by the window to contemplate her next move.
He contemplated quitting his job to start a business.

meanings.definition: To consider the possibility of doing something.
examples: They are contemplating a move to the countryside.

meanings.definition: Formal forgiveness of a person's sins or wrongdoing.
examples: The priest granted him absolution after his confession.
She sought absolution for the harm she had caused.

meanings.definition: To describe or show something precisely, as by drawing or outlining it.
examples: The map clearly delineates the boundaries of each district.
The report delineates the steps needed to reach the goal.

meanings.definition: A pipe, tube, or channel for carrying water, fluid, or wires.
examples: The water flowed through an underground conduit to the reservoir.
Electrical cables ran through a metal conduit in the wall.

meanings.definition: A person or organization that acts as a channel for passing something along.
examples: The charity served as a conduit for donations to the disaster zone.

meanings.definition: Not living permanently in a particular place or jurisdiction.
examples: Non-resident students pay higher tuition fees.
He filed a non-resident tax return for the state.

meanings.definition: The quality of lasting only a short time.
examples: The poem captures the transience of youth and beauty.
She was struck by the transience of fame.

meanings.definition: To compete eagerly with others to achieve or win something.
examples: Several candidates are vying for the leadership position.
The two teams vied for the championship title.

meanings.definition: Persistently and urgently demanding or asking for something.
examples: The importunate beggar would not leave until he was given money.
She grew tired of his importunate requests for help.

meanings.definition: To change the structure, shape, or appearance of something, especially a building or room.
examples: They plan to remodel the kitchen this summer.
The old factory was remodeled into modern apartments.

meanings.definition: So small as to be unimportant; trivial in amount.
examples: The museum charges only a nominal fee for entry.
He paid a nominal sum for the antique chair.

meanings.definition: Existing in name only and not in reality.
examples: She was the nominal head of the company, but others made the decisions.

meanings.definition: The activity or skill of forming letters and words on a surface.
examples: Writing by hand is becoming a lost skill.
Her writing is so neat that it looks printed.

meanings.definition: Written works such as books, articles, or other texts.
examples: The writings of ancient philosophers still influence us today.

meanings.definition: Able to be folded or pressed down into a smaller, flatter shape.
examples: She packed a collapsible umbrella in her bag.
The collapsible chairs were stored against the wall.

meanings.definition: To pave a road or path with layers of small broken stones.
examples: The workers macadamized the country lane in the 1800s.
Once they macadamized the road, travel became much smoother.

meanings.definition: Not hopeful or encouraging; gloomy and depressing.
examples: The future looked bleak after the factory closed.
Doctors gave a bleak prognosis for his recovery.

meanings.definition: Cold, bare, and exposed to the weather.
examples: A bleak wind swept across the empty moor.

meanings.definition: An injury that does not break the skin but causes a bruise; a bruise.
examples: The fall left a painful contusion on her knee.
X-rays showed no fractures, only contusions.

meanings.definition: Completely fascinated by and absorbed in something.
examples: The children listened with rapt attention to the storyteller.
She gazed at the stage with a rapt expression.

meanings.definition: A pair of letters that together represent a single sound, such as 'sh' or 'ph'.
examples: The word 'phone' begins with the digraph 'ph'.
Teachers introduce digraphs like 'ch' and 'th' early in reading lessons.

meanings.definition: To use something wrongly or treat someone or something badly.
examples: He was accused of misusing public funds.
Employees who misuse company equipment may be fired.

meanings.definition: To forbid or condemn something, especially officially.
examples: The new law proscribes the sale of these chemicals.
Many cultures proscribe certain foods for religious reasons.

meanings.definition: The specialized vocabulary or jargon of a particular group or field.
examples: I couldn't follow the legal lingo the lawyers were using.
Every sport has its own lingo that newcomers must learn.

meanings.definition: A conclusion reached on the basis of evidence and reasoning.
examples: From her silence, he drew the inference that she disagreed.
Scientists made an inference about the climate from the ice samples.

meanings.definition: Producing a large amount of goods, results, or work.
examples: The meeting was productive, and we solved several problems.
Fertile soil makes the land highly productive.

meanings.definition: A state of physical or mental inactivity, dullness, or apathy.
examples: The summer heat left everyone in a state of torpor.
She struggled to shake off the torpor of the long afternoon.

meanings.definition: A person or thing that takes the place of another that came before.
examples: The king named his eldest son as his successor.
The new model is a worthy successor to the original.

meanings.definition: To give someone support, confidence, or hope.
examples: Her teacher encouraged her to pursue a career in science.
We try to encourage students to ask questions.

meanings.definition: To make something more likely to happen or develop.
examples: Warm weather encourages the growth of weeds.

meanings.definition: A newspaper or magazine article expressing the editor's or publication's opinion on an issue.
examples: The newspaper ran an editorial criticizing the new tax law.
She wrote a powerful editorial about education reform.

meanings.definition: A sharp, harsh, squeaking sound, as made by a hinge or floorboard.
examples: The old door opened with a loud creak.
Every step on the stairs produced a creak.

meanings.definition: Existing, happening, or used beneath the surface of the sea.
examples: A submarine cable carries data across the ocean floor.
Scientists studied the submarine volcano from a research ship.

meanings.definition: To bring people together for a meeting, or to come together formally.
examples: The committee will convene next week to vote on the proposal.
Leaders convened to discuss the crisis.

meanings.definition: A strange or unusual quality, or a person or thing that is strange.
examples: His habit of talking to plants was seen as a harmless oddity.
The shop was full of oddities collected from around the world.

meanings.definition: To say or do something that removes someone's doubts or fears.
examples: The nurse reassured the nervous patient before the operation.
He reassured me that everything would be fine.

meanings.definition: An order or law that forbids something.
examples: There is a strict prohibition against smoking in the building.
The prohibition on imports hurt local businesses.

meanings.definition: Plowed land that is left unplanted for a season to restore its fertility.
examples: The farmer left the north field in fallow for a year.
Rotating crops and fallow keeps the soil healthy.

meanings.definition: Left unseeded after plowing; not currently in use.
examples: The fallow fields stretched out under the autumn sky.

meanings.definition: A critical or censorious remark.
examples: His speech contained several harsh animadversions on the policy.
She resented the animadversions cast on her work.

meanings.definition: A release or discharge from a debt or obligation, or a document proving it.
examples: The receipt served as an acquittance for the full payment.
He demanded a written acquittance before settling the account.

meanings.definition: The act of announcing or formally proclaiming something.
examples: The annunciation of the prize winner was met with applause.
Bells rang to mark the annunciation of the royal birth.

meanings.definition: Done by accident and not on purpose; unintentional.
examples: An inadvertent error in the report caused some confusion.
She made an inadvertent slip that revealed the surprise.

meanings.definition: To last or survive longer than something or someone else.
examples: These boots will outlast any cheaper pair.
The ancient bridge has outlasted many newer structures.

meanings.definition: The act of doing or saying something again.
examples: Learning a language requires a lot of repetition.
The constant repetition of the song made it stick in my head.

meanings.definition: To attack a place continuously with bombs or gunfire.
examples: Enemy planes bombarded the city for hours.
The fortress was bombarded until its walls fell.

meanings.definition: To direct a large number of things, such as questions or criticism, at someone.
examples: Reporters bombarded the senator with questions.

meanings.definition: A gentle but firm warning or piece of advice.
examples: She ignored her mother's admonition to drive carefully.
The coach's admonition about teamwork stayed with the players.

meanings.definition: A stream or river that flows into and joins another.
examples: The river has several confluents that swell it after the rains.
Each confluent adds to the volume of the main channel.

meanings.definition: The quality of being strict, severe, or demanding.
examples: The stringency of the new rules surprised the staff.
Financial stringency forced the company to cut costs.

meanings.definition: A perfect or typical example of a kind of person or thing; an original model.
examples: He is the archetype of the self-made entrepreneur.
The story uses the archetype of the heroic journey.

meanings.definition: A speech delivered, or meant to be delivered, with formal, dramatic expression in public.
examples: The students prepared a declamation for the school assembly.
His fiery declamation moved the entire audience.

meanings.definition: To make someone drunk or intensely excited.
examples: Too much wine can quickly inebriate a person.
The roaring crowd seemed to inebriate the young performer.

meanings.definition: A person who behaves badly or breaks the law; a villain.
examples: The police rounded up the miscreants responsible for the vandalism.
The teacher scolded the miscreants who had skipped class.

meanings.definition: The science and art of teaching.
examples: She studied pedagogics before becoming a primary school teacher.
Modern pedagogics emphasizes active learning over memorization.

meanings.definition: An attitude of distrust that doubts the sincerity or value of others' motives.
examples: Years of broken promises bred a deep cynicism in him.
Her cynicism made it hard for her to trust new colleagues.

meanings.definition: A great number or wide variety of something.
examples: The city offers a multiplicity of cultural attractions.
A multiplicity of factors contributed to the company's failure.

meanings.definition: A speech given as a farewell, especially by a graduating student.
examples: She delivered the valedictory at her college graduation.
His valedictory brought many in the audience to tears.

meanings.definition: To tell someone about a private matter while trusting them to keep it secret.
examples: She confided her fears to her closest friend.
He confided that he was planning to resign.

meanings.definition: A device with rotating blades that drives a ship or aircraft forward.
examples: The plane's propeller spun faster as it prepared for takeoff.
A bent propeller slowed the boat to a crawl.

meanings.definition: A person who is united with others in an alliance, plot, or agreement.
examples: The thief and his confederates planned the robbery for weeks.
She refused to become a confederate in their scheme.

meanings.definition: To send someone away from their native country, or to leave one's own country to live elsewhere.
examples: The regime expatriated thousands of political dissidents.
After the war, he chose to expatriate himself and settle in Paris.

meanings.definition: Relating to marriage or a wedding ceremony.
examples: The couple exchanged nuptial vows in a garden ceremony.
Guests gathered for the nuptial celebration.

meanings.definition: The branch of science that studies fossils and ancient forms of life.
examples: Paleontology has revealed much about how dinosaurs lived.
She devoted her career to paleontology and the study of fossils.

meanings.definition: A person who gives money or a gift, especially to a charity or institution.
examples: The museum thanked the donator of the rare painting.
Each donator received a letter of appreciation.

meanings.definition: A covered channel or large pipe that carries water under a road, railway, or embankment.
examples: Heavy rain caused the culvert beneath the road to overflow.
Workers cleared the blocked culvert to prevent flooding.

meanings.definition: A lack of respect for things that are usually treated as sacred or important.
examples: His irreverence toward authority often got him into trouble.
The comedy is famous for its irreverence and bold jokes.

meanings.definition: Fully in agreement; held or shared by everyone involved.
examples: The jury reached a unanimous verdict.
The decision to expand was unanimous among the board members.

meanings.definition: The act of following or complying with rules, standards, or expectations.
examples: The building was inspected for conformance with safety codes.
Their products are tested for conformance to industry standards.

meanings.definition: Genuine and of undisputed origin; real, not a copy.
examples: The gallery confirmed that the painting was authentic.
The restaurant serves authentic Italian cuisine.

meanings.definition: True to one's own personality or character; sincere.
examples: People appreciated her authentic and honest manner.

meanings.definition: Having the power to make laws, or relating to such a process.
examples: Congress holds the legislative power of the federal government.
The bill passed through both legislative chambers.

meanings.definition: The female of the deer, as well as of the rabbit and certain other animals.
examples: A doe and her fawn grazed at the edge of the forest.
The hunters were not permitted to shoot a doe.

meanings.definition: Full of high-spirited fun; playful and lively.
examples: The frolicsome puppies chased each other around the yard.
She was in a frolicsome mood and joked with everyone.

meanings.definition: Loud or clear enough to be heard.
examples: Her voice was barely audible over the roar of the crowd.
He gave an audible sigh of relief when the results came in.

meanings.definition: Written works, especially those considered to have lasting artistic or intellectual value.
examples: She devoted her life to the study of nineteenth-century literature.
Shakespeare's plays are a cornerstone of English literature.

meanings.definition: Printed material on a particular subject, such as leaflets or brochures.
examples: The clinic handed out literature on healthy eating.
I picked up some literature about the new software at the conference.

meanings.definition: A breaking apart of people or groups once joined, especially a loss of unity or shared interest.
examples: Years of resentment finally led to the disunion of the once-allied states.
The committee feared that disagreement over funding would cause lasting disunion.

meanings.definition: The branch of biology that deals with the normal functions of living organisms and their parts.
examples: She studied human physiology to understand how the heart pumps blood.
Exercise physiology explains why muscles tire during a long run.

meanings.definition: Too small or gradual to be perceived.
examples: The temperature dropped by insensible degrees as evening came on.
There was an almost insensible shift in his tone that few noticed.

meanings.definition: Unconscious or unable to feel something.
examples: The blow left him insensible on the floor.
She seemed insensible to the danger she was in.

meanings.definition: To transform something into a more beautiful, exalted, or glorified form.
examples: Joy seemed to transfigure her ordinary face.
The morning light transfigured the dull street into something radiant.

meanings.definition: A person from a country other than one's own.
examples: As a foreigner, he found the local customs confusing at first.
The festival welcomes both locals and foreigners every summer.

meanings.definition: To collect and store away a large supply of something, often secretly or greedily.
examples: During the shortage, people began to hoard canned food.
The miser hoarded gold coins beneath the floorboards.

meanings.definition: A pause or reluctance caused by uncertainty before acting or speaking.
examples: There was a slight hesitancy in her voice when she answered.
He agreed without the least hesitancy.

meanings.definition: Latin as used since the Renaissance, especially in scholarship and scientific naming.
examples: Many scientific species names are coined in neo-Latin.
The botanist published his findings in neo-Latin so scholars everywhere could read them.

meanings.definition: To come out into view or come into existence from a hidden or unclear state.
examples: The sun emerged from behind the clouds.
A new leader emerged during the crisis.

meanings.definition: A person who studies and is an expert in religion and the nature of God.
examples: The theologian wrote a famous book on the problem of suffering.
Medieval theologians debated the meaning of grace for centuries.

meanings.definition: A loss of moral purity or integrity, especially dishonest conduct by those in power.
examples: The scandal exposed widespread corruption in the city government.
Bribery and corruption had eaten away at public trust.

meanings.definition: The process of decay or the introduction of errors, as in a text or data.
examples: A power failure caused corruption of the saved files.
Centuries of copying introduced corruption into the ancient manuscript.

meanings.definition: To burn or scorch the surface of something, often with sudden intense heat.
examples: Sear the steak quickly on both sides to lock in the juices.
The hot iron seared the cloth before she could pull it away.

meanings.definition: Producing a good or beneficial effect, especially on health or well-being.
examples: The defeat had a salutary effect, forcing the team to train harder.
A walk in the fresh air can have a salutary influence on the mind.

meanings.definition: Not seeming reasonable or likely to be true.
examples: His excuse for being late was utterly implausible.
The plot relied on an implausible series of coincidences.

meanings.definition: The art or skill of planning and carrying out actions to achieve a particular goal.
examples: The coach changed his tactics in the second half.
Negotiation often depends on clever tactics rather than brute force.

meanings.definition: The arrangement and maneuvering of military forces in battle.
examples: The general was renowned for his daring battlefield tactics.
Modern warfare requires both strategy and flexible tactics.

meanings.definition: A woman who flirts playfully to win admiration without serious intentions.
examples: She was a charming coquette who delighted in turning men's heads.
Everyone knew the young coquette enjoyed teasing her many suitors.

meanings.definition: The merging of two adjacent vowels or syllables into one, as in shortening "ever" to "e'er".
examples: The poet used syneresis to fit the line into the meter.
Through syneresis, two short syllables are pronounced as a single one.

meanings.definition: Friendly and generous in welcoming and entertaining guests or strangers.
examples: Our hosts were warm and hospitable throughout our stay.
The villagers were hospitable to every traveler who passed through.

meanings.definition: The coining or use of new words or of new meanings for existing words.
examples: The rise of the internet has driven a wave of neology.
Critics dismissed his neology as needless jargon.

meanings.definition: A person or animal born with little or no pigment, having very pale skin and hair and light-sensitive eyes.
examples: The albino rabbit had snow-white fur and pink eyes.
Born an albino, she had to shield her skin carefully from the sun.

meanings.definition: Too large or great to be measured.
examples: The night sky stretched out in immeasurable vastness.
Her contribution to the project was of immeasurable value.

meanings.definition: To spoil the appearance of something by marking or damaging its surface.
examples: Vandals defaced the monument with spray paint.
He was fined for defacing public property.

meanings.definition: An official ban on trade or commercial activity with a particular country.
examples: The government imposed an embargo on oil exports.
The trade embargo crippled the small nation's economy.

meanings.definition: To repeatedly demand payment of a debt from someone.
examples: Collectors dunned him daily for the unpaid loan.
She was tired of being dunned for bills she had already settled.

meanings.definition: A short phrase or sentence expressing a guiding principle or ideal.
examples: "Be prepared" is the motto of the Scouts.
The family motto urged honesty above all else.

meanings.definition: To regard or represent something as perfect or better than it really is.
examples: People often idealize their childhood as a time of pure happiness.
The film idealizes country life and ignores its hardships.

meanings.definition: An oval or round open-air building with tiers of seats surrounding a central arena.
examples: The Roman amphitheater could hold thousands of spectators.
Crowds gathered in the amphitheater to watch the contests.

meanings.definition: Pleasant-sounding because of a sweet, tuneful succession of notes.
examples: The melodious song of the nightingale filled the garden.
She had a soft, melodious voice that soothed everyone.

meanings.definition: Wisely preparing for the future, especially by saving or planning ahead.
examples: A provident farmer stores grain for the lean months.
Being provident with her earnings, she retired without worry.

meanings.definition: A portable musical instrument played by squeezing a bellows while pressing keys or buttons.
examples: He played a lively folk tune on his accordion.
The accordion wheezed out a waltz in the village square.

meanings.definition: Sharply sarcastic or biting in speech or manner.
examples: The critic was famous for his mordacious reviews.
Her mordacious wit could wound even the most confident speaker.

meanings.definition: The criminal world, made up of organized crime and those who operate outside the law.
examples: The detective spent years infiltrating the city's underworld.
He had connections deep within the criminal underworld.

meanings.definition: In mythology, the world of the dead beneath the earth.
examples: In Greek myth, Hades ruled the underworld.
Orpheus journeyed to the underworld to bring back his wife.

meanings.definition: A planned route or detailed schedule of a journey.
examples: Our itinerary included three days in Rome and two in Florence.
She emailed the travel itinerary to everyone in the group.

meanings.definition: To cause a woman or female animal to become pregnant.
examples: The mare was impregnated by the prize stallion.
Only one of the eggs was successfully impregnated.

meanings.definition: To soak or saturate something thoroughly with a substance.
examples: The cloth was impregnated with a waterproof coating.
Smoke had impregnated every fiber of his jacket.

meanings.definition: A written message or letter, especially a long or formal one.
examples: She received a lengthy missive from her old friend.
The general sent a stern missive to his commanders.

meanings.definition: The pleasure or satisfaction that comes from having a desire fulfilled.
examples: He delayed the gratification of dessert until he finished his work.
There was deep gratification in seeing the project finally succeed.

meanings.definition: Having qualities that make a person or thing easy to love.
examples: The puppy was a clumsy but lovable little creature.
Despite his flaws, he remained a lovable character.

meanings.definition: Excessive concern with being or appearing proper, modest, or easily shocked.
examples: Her prudery made her blush at the mildest joke.
The novel was banned out of sheer Victorian prudery.
